metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC7: State the maximum SIGTERM drain timeout and set a matching `stop_grace_period` in the compose service (e.g., `stop_grace_period: 5m`) so the drain bound is explicit and Docker doesn't SIGKILL prematurely.
findings.finding: AC7 specifies 'finish the current wipChain' on SIGTERM but states no maximum drain timeout, leaving the executor to decide how long the service may block a redeploy — a defaultable gap that must be stated to be actionable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7: 'Set `stop_grace_period: 5m` in the compose service; the bot must exit within this window or be force-killed. Log a warning if the wipChain cannot complete in time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit work item to modify `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` to fetch `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` → `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` and write it to `.env`, analogous to the existing `gerrit-bot-token` entry.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` does NOT fetch `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token`. The plan states the token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but the live script explicitly enumerates its 7 fixed fetches (lines 11-25, 81-91) and the autolander token is absent. The plan never states that fetch-secrets.sh must be modified as part of this story's scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` in the fetch block and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}"` in the .env write block of fetch-secrets.sh.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly scope a new section in `infra/scripts/observability.sh` (following the §4/§4c/§4d offset-delta pattern) that greps `compose-autolander-1` journald for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` AND checks container health state, publishing `rebar/host:autolander_errors`.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/observability.sh` does NOT currently publish `rebar/host:autolander_errors` or contain any reference to `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` or `compose-autolander-1`. The plan's AC3 states observability.sh 'publishes the single `rebar/host:autolander_errors` metric … from BOTH feeders', presenting this as the existing mechanism rather than new work to extend the script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` as an explicit edit target: extend `SITE_SUBDIRS` to include the autolander state-volume subdir so `config-check.sh` check 5 passes and the EBS-backed bind volume is provisioned on boot.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is an unenumerated edit target: its `SITE_SUBDIRS` variable is the single source of truth for autolander state-volume provisioning. Adding an `external: true` autolander volume to `docker-compose.yml` without extending `SITE_SUBDIRS` will cause `config-check.sh` check 5 (the incident-2731 drift gate) to fail CI and `compose up` to error at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander` (or similar) to `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh`, and declare the corresponding `gerrit_autolander` volume as `external: true` in `docker-compose.yml` with the matching entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly list `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` as an edit target in the plan and specify the new `get_param autolander-gerrit-token` → `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` mapping line to add.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` is referenced in AC2 as the fetcher for `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token`, but it is not explicitly listed as an edit target requiring a new SSM-leaf → env-var mapping entry. The current script has a fixed, explicitly enumerated mapping table; `autolander-gerrit-token` is absent from it and must be added as a concrete code change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}"` to the fetch-secrets.sh mapping table, mirroring the pattern used for `gerrit-bot-token`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for detecting container 'unhealthy' state in observability.sh: either '`docker inspect --format={{.State.Health.Status}} compose-autolander-1`' (requires docker socket access for the probe) or '`docker events --filter event=health_status --filter container=compose-autolander-1`' — and confirm the probe has the necessary permissions, since the existing grep-journald pattern does not cover Docker engine health-state events.
findings.finding: AC3's 'container unhealthy state' feeder for the autolander_errors metric specifies it is 'grepped on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1' — but Docker's unhealthy health state is NOT emitted as a journald log line under CONTAINER_NAME. The existing pattern (voter_errors, g2p errors) greps journald for structured log markers emitted by the process itself. Container health state transitions are Docker-engine events, not journald entries, so the grep-journald mechanism used for all other feeders does not apply here. The observability.sh probe would need 'docker inspect' or 'docker events' polling instead — a different probe shape the plan does not specify.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a bounded drain timeout to AC7: specify a 'stop_grace_period' for the compose service (e.g. matching the wipChain-timeout value from S2/S3) AND a loop-level drain limit (e.g. 'if SIGTERM and phase=awaiting_verified AND time-in-phase > T, park the wipChain and exit') so the drain completes within the grace period rather than being SIGKILL'd.
findings.finding: AC7 specifies drain-on-SIGTERM ('finish the current wipChain, ack its outcome, then exit') but provides no timeout bound on the drain. Docker compose's default stop timeout is 10 seconds before SIGKILL; a wipChain in 'awaiting_verified' phase could be waiting hours for a CI run. Without an explicit 'stop_grace_period' in the compose service definition AND a bounded drain timeout in the loop (e.g. 'if SIGTERM received and phase is awaiting_verified, park the wipChain and exit rather than waiting indefinitely'), the drain will be silently killed by SIGKILL in 10 seconds on every redeploy, defeating the stated goal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide and specify the heartbeat mechanism in AC5: choose one option (timestamp file is simpler and avoids AWS creds in the loop), name the concrete file path (e.g., `/var/lib/autolander/heartbeat`), and specify the staleness threshold (e.g., 'stale if > 2× poll interval') — so S4's `lander_down` detection can be implemented against a fixed contract.
findings.finding: AC5 presents two mutually exclusive heartbeat implementation options ('a timestamp file in the state volume (or a health metric)') with no decision, leaving the executor to choose mid-implementation. S4's `lander_down` outcome reads this signal, so the mechanism, path/name, and staleness threshold are load-bearing for a cross-story contract.
findings.location: AC5 (Heartbeat): 'a timestamp file in the state volume (or a health metric)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete detection protocol for the `unhealthy` container-state feeder in AC3: either define the exact `docker inspect --format` or `docker events` command that surfaces `unhealthy` and how it maps to a metric increment, or describe the journald entry pattern that the Docker daemon writes when HEALTHCHECK transitions to `unhealthy` (e.g., `logger -t docker …` pattern) — analogous to the explicit grep patterns in existing observability.sh sections.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies that `observability.sh` detects the container's `unhealthy` state 'grepped on `CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1`' but provides no grep pattern or detection protocol — unlike every existing section of `observability.sh` (sections 3, 4, 4b, 4c, 4d) which each specify an exact grep pattern (e.g., `VOTER_ERROR`, `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR`, `G2P_PATTERN`). Container health state is not in journald logs in the same format as service markers; the detection mechanism is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1` to check health and emit a metric increment when the result is `unhealthy`; this is deterministic unlike grepping journald for Docker health-state changes.

findings.finding: `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` is named as the fetch mechanism for `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` but is not listed as an edit target. The file currently has a fixed set of `get_param` calls and `.env` write lines; adding the new SSM leaf requires a concrete code addition to this file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line in the plan's edit-targets: 'Edit `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`: add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}"` to the .env write block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the autolander state volume (lockfile + heartbeat) is declared `external: true` in docker-compose.yml and provisioned by compose-up.sh as a bind-mount onto an EBS-backed path (e.g. /var/gerrit/site/autolander), consistent with all other stateful volumes.
findings.finding: The autolander state volume — which holds both the POSIX flock file (AC1 single-instance guard) and the heartbeat timestamp (AC5) — has no specified lifecycle: the plan never says it is declared `external: true` in docker-compose.yml, never says compose-up.sh provisions it as a bind-mount onto the EBS-backed /var/gerrit/site/* path, and never says whether it survives `docker compose down -v`. All existing stateful volumes (gerrit_git, gerrit_reviewbot, etc.) are external:true and bound to EBS by compose-up.sh; the plan leaves the autolander volume's durability unspecified.
findings.location: AC1 (single-instance guard / flock on lockfile in state volume), AC5 (heartbeat timestamp file in state volume)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to docker-compose.yml under `volumes:` an entry `gerrit_autolander: { external: true }`, mount it into the container at `/var/lib/autolander`, and add the corresponding `docker volume create` bind step in compose-up.sh targeting `/var/gerrit/site/autolander`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` is added to `local.rebar_secret_params` in ssm.tf (inheriting `ignore_changes = [value]` and the CHANGEME sentinel) AND that fetch-secrets.sh gains the mapping `autolander-gerrit-token -> AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`.
findings.finding: The plan says the SSM parameter is 'added in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`' and 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but the existing ssm.tf manages all secrets via a single `for_each = toset(local.rebar_secret_params)` resource with `ignore_changes = [value]` and a CHANGEME sentinel (enforced by user_data.sh), and fetch-secrets.sh has an explicit curated mapping of SSM leaf → env-var. The plan does not specify: (a) adding '/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token' to `local.rebar_secret_params` vs. a standalone aws_ssm_parameter resource, or (b) adding the mapping entry to fetch-secrets.sh (e.g. `autolander-gerrit-token -> AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`). A standalone resource without `ignore_changes = [value]` would revert operator-set values on each apply; omitting fetch-secrets.sh means the token is never materialized into the container.
findings.location: AC2 (credentials mechanism): 'added in infra/terraform/ssm.tf, fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh'
findings.suggested_fix: In ssm.tf add `"/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token"` to the `rebar_secret_params` list. In fetch-secrets.sh add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` in the required-params block and `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}` in the .env write block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete provisioning mechanism for the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` All-Projects capability grant in AC2: specify which script (a new `setup-autolander-acls.sh` or an extension of `setup-project.sh`) pushes the `[capability]` stanza to `All-Projects/refs/meta/config`, and include the exact config snippet (e.g. `[access] / rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`) so the implementer can verify it against live Gerrit 3.14 docs.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` under `[capability]`, but no provisioning script or config snippet for this grant is specified, and there is zero prior use of `[capability]` blocks in the repo's `infra/gerrit/` scripts. The `setup-project.sh` only manages the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`, not `All-Projects`. This is a new integration surface — Unverified at the config-syntax and provisioning-path level — and a capability gap: the plan names the requirement but does not name which script adds it or provide the exact `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Explicitly declare the env-var name for the autolander Gerrit token (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`) and confirm that `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` is updated to add the `autolander-gerrit-token` → `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` SSM-leaf→env-var mapping and write it to the `.env` consumed by the autolander compose service.
findings.finding: The plan states the Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh' but does not declare (a) the env-var name under which the container receives the token, (b) that fetch-secrets.sh is updated to include the new SSM leaf→env-var mapping, or (c) whether the container reads it from the shared .env or a separate mechanism. The existing fetch-secrets.sh has no autolander-gerrit-token entry. An undeclared secret delivery path for a boundary-crossing credential (the token grants submit rights) is itself a security gap — if the mapping is omitted, the container starts with no token and the gap might be papered over with a less-secure fallback.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to fetch-secrets.sh's required-params block: `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` and add `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}"` to the .env write block. Name the var in AC2 so the Dockerfile and loop code have a single canonical reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the emergency-stop sentinel's full lifecycle in AC7: concrete file path (e.g. `/state/emergency-stop`), who creates/removes it (operator runbook step), whether container recreation clears it, and that removal is atomic (e.g. `rm` is atomic on POSIX for a plain sentinel file).
findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel (AC7) has no defined CREATE/REMOVE lifecycle: the plan doesn't specify who creates the sentinel file to pause the bot, where exactly it lives relative to the state volume mount path, how an operator removes it to resume normal operation, or whether there is any guard preventing the file from persisting across container recreation (which would leave the bot permanently paused after a redeploy).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the heartbeat staleness threshold in AC5 (e.g. 'stale if >N×poll_interval seconds old, where poll_interval is defined in S2') so S4 and the monitoring alarm use the same concrete value.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file's staleness threshold is not defined for its named consumer (S4's `lander_down` outcome). The plan says 'a stale heartbeat means the bot is down/wedged' and that S4 reads it, but does not specify what age (seconds/minutes) constitutes stale, leaving the consumer's tolerance undefined and making the signal ambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve AC4's restart-on-unhealthy mechanism concretely: either (a) add a `willfarrell/autoheal` (or equivalent) sidecar service to `docker-compose.yml` that watches for `unhealthy` state and restarts the autolander, or (b) replace the HEALTHCHECK-based approach with a watchdog systemd unit on the host that checks `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}'` and calls `docker compose restart autolander`, naming the exact mechanism in AC4.
findings.finding: AC4 asserts 'autoheal/restart: unless-stopped' will restart the container on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy`, but plain `docker compose` `restart: unless-stopped` fires only on process EXIT — it does NOT restart a still-running but `unhealthy` container. No autoheal sidecar (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal`) is present in the existing `docker-compose.yml` or planned concretely in this story. This is a Contradicted capability: the named mechanism cannot deliver the stated restart-on-unhealthy behaviour without an additional component.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `docker-compose.yml` alongside the autolander service:
```yaml
autoheal:
  image: willfarrell/autoheal:latest
  restart: unless-stopped
  volumes:
    - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
  environment:
    AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL: autoheal
```
And add `labels: [autoheal=true]` to the autolander service. Then update AC4 to name `willfarrell/autoheal` as the concrete mechanism.

findings.finding: The plan asserts the autolander Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but reading `fetch-secrets.sh` shows it has a hardcoded leaf list (lines 81–91 of the file) that does NOT include `autolander-gerrit-token`. The script must be explicitly modified to add the new leaf — this is not covered by existing code.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, replace 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`' with 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (add `autolander-gerrit-token → AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` to the script's hardcoded leaf list at lines 81–91 and the corresponding output line)'.

findings.finding: The plan specifies the autolander Gerrit token is 'fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh' but does not specify adding `autolander-gerrit-token` to the script's curated SSM-leaf → env-var mapping. The existing script has an explicit, closed list of `get_param` calls and a matching `.env` write block; without a concrete addition, the SSM SecureString at `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` will exist in Terraform but will never be read or written to the container's `.env`, leaving the container without its credential at runtime.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials) — 'fetched by infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh'; also infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh lines 81–108
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's AC2 paragraph, add: 'Also add `autolander_gerrit_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-token)"` to `fetch-secrets.sh` (required, not optional) and `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_token}` to the `.env` write block.'

findings.finding: The plan names `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level capability to be granted in `All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability]`, but does not specify any provisioning artifact (script, operator runbook, or Terraform resource) that actually adds this grant. The existing repo has service-user.sh for the rebar project's webhooks.config and setup-project.sh for the rebar project's refs/meta/config — neither touches All-Projects. There is no existing script that edits All-Projects refs/meta/config, and adding a capability there requires admin SSH access and a separate refs/meta/config push to the All-Projects repository.
findings.location: AC2 (Credentials) — '`rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` (`[capability]` / service-user grant)'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is granted as a `[capability]` stanza in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config`, but no existing script or config in the repo provisions `All-Projects` capabilities — only the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config` is managed (via `setup-project.sh`). The exact config key for this capability on Gerrit 3.14.1 and the delivery script/mechanism are both unspecified, leaving the capability grant unverified and undelivered.

AC3 specifies a two-feeder `autolander_errors` metric (AUTOLANDER_ERROR journald grep + container `unhealthy` state) but does not specify how the `unhealthy` container state is observable in journald. Unlike the AUTOLANDER_ERROR feeder (stdout/stderr → journald), Docker health-check state transitions (`unhealthy`) appear in `docker events` output, not in the container's journald stream under CONTAINER_NAME. The plan says both feeders go through `infra/scripts/observability.sh` but gives no implementation protocol for the `unhealthy` feeder, unlike every peer section in observability.sh which fully specifies its source and pattern.
findings.location: AC3 (Observability): 'the container's unhealthy state (grepped on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a bounded drain timeout for SIGTERM handling (e.g., a configurable `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S` env var, with a corresponding `stop_grace_period` in the compose service) so the container either finishes drain within the bound or exits cleanly with a logged warning.
findings.finding: The drain-on-SIGTERM contract ('finish the current wipChain, ack its outcome, then exit') has no timeout bound. If a wipChain is stuck or takes longer than Docker's stop-timeout (default 10 s), the container will be SIGKILL'd mid-drain — the very stranded-stack scenario the drain is meant to prevent — or, if the stop-timeout is raised, `docker compose down` hangs indefinitely and blocks deployments.
findings.location: AC7 — 'Drain-on-shutdown: on redeploy/SIGTERM, finish the current wipChain, ack its outcome, then exit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `stop_grace_period: 120s` (or a chosen bound) to the compose service definition, and implement the SIGTERM handler as: attempt drain for up to `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_S` seconds, then log a warning and exit 1 so the `autolander_errors` metric fires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in AC3 that `infra/scripts/observability.sh` must be updated with a new autolander feeder section (AUTOLANDER_ERROR journald grep + unhealthy state grep on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1), using the same offset-file delta pattern as existing feeders.
findings.finding: The plan describes `infra/scripts/observability.sh` publishing the `autolander_errors` metric from two feeders (AUTOLANDER_ERROR journald marker + container unhealthy state grep on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1), but does not specify that observability.sh must be modified — it describes what the script will publish, not the change to the file. The existing observability.sh has no autolander section. Without an explicit call-out that the script requires new feeder sections (with the same offset-file delta pattern used for all other feeders), an implementer might add the metric only to the Dockerfile/container rather than the host probe, breaking the two-feeder design.
findings.location: AC3: 'infra/scripts/observability.sh publishes the single rebar/host:autolander_errors metric ... from BOTH feeders'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC2's Gerrit config section reference: replace `[capability]` with `[access "Global Capabilities"]` and provide the exact stanza (e.g. `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`) or point to a setup script (analogous to infra/gerrit/service-user.sh) that pushes the grant to All-Projects refs/meta/config.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 asserts that `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is granted via `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` under a `[capability]` section, but Gerrit's actual configuration format uses `[access "Global Capabilities"]` as the section header for global capability grants (e.g. `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`). A `[capability]` section does not exist in Gerrit's `project.config` schema — an implementer following the plan's literal wording will produce a silently-ignored or rejected config block, leaving the bot without the capability.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, replace the parenthetical with: 'granted in All-Projects refs/meta/config under `[access "Global Capabilities"]` — e.g. `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`; add a setup script (analogous to service-user.sh) that fetches All-Projects refs/meta/config, appends the stanza, and pushes it back.'

findings.finding: The plan's AC3 observability mechanism for the `unhealthy`-state feeder is technically infeasible as described. AC3 states the `unhealthy` container state is detected by observability.sh 'grepped on CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1'. The existing observability.sh uses `journalctl CONTAINER_NAME=<name>` to scan container stdout/stderr log lines. Docker health-state transitions (`healthy` → `unhealthy`) are emitted as Docker daemon events via `docker events`, NOT as stdout/stderr lines shipped to journald under `CONTAINER_NAME=`. journald will not contain an `unhealthy` token from a Docker health state change; it will only contain what the container's HEALTHCHECK command writes to stdout/stderr.
findings.suggested_fix: In observability.sh, add a stanza that calls `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1 2>/dev/null` and publishes `autolander_errors += 1` when the result is `unhealthy`. This is synchronous, uses the already-present Docker CLI, and requires no daemon-socket sidecar. Update AC3 to specify `docker inspect` as the unhealthy-state detection method.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in AC4/AC5 (and in Dockerfile.autolander) that the HEALTHCHECK script checks heartbeat file staleness (e.g., `test $(( $(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y /state/heartbeat) )) -lt MAX_STALE_S`) rather than process liveness alone.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK's mechanism for detecting a hung poll loop is unspecified. AC4 states the HEALTHCHECK 'marks the container unhealthy on a hang', and AC5 introduces a heartbeat timestamp file written on every poll iteration — but the plan never explicitly connects them. If the HEALTHCHECK script does not check heartbeat staleness (e.g., if it only checks process liveness or a trivial endpoint), a wedged poll loop will never be detected as `unhealthy`, the autoheal/restart will not fire, and the `autolander_errors` metric will not increment.
findings.suggested_fix: In `Dockerfile.autolander`, add: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=10s --retries=3 CMD [ "sh", "-c", "test $(( $(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y /state/heartbeat) )) -lt 180" ]` and define `MAX_POLL_INTERVAL_S` (configurable env var) so the staleness threshold scales with the configured poll cadence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the concrete autoheal mechanism in AC4: either (a) add a `willfarrell/autoheal` (or equivalent) sidecar to docker-compose.yml that watches `unhealthy` containers and restarts them, or (b) document that the HEALTHCHECK causes the container to self-terminate on hang (e.g. the health-check script kills the main process) so `restart: unless-stopped` can take over — and verify the chosen approach actually fires on `unhealthy` in plain `docker compose`.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 'autoheal/restart: unless-stopped' mechanism for restarting on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy` is an unverified capability assertion: plain `docker compose` `restart: unless-stopped` fires only on process EXIT, not on `unhealthy` HEALTHCHECK state. Without a sidecar like `willfarrell/autoheal` (or equivalent), an `unhealthy` container that is still running will NOT be restarted. The plan names 'autoheal' but does not commit to a concrete implementation or verify that the chosen mechanism actually handles the `unhealthy` → restart path in plain (non-Swarm) compose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated autoheal sidecar (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal:latest` with `AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autolander` and the autolander service labeled `autoheal=true`) to docker-compose.yml, following the established pattern; or make the HEALTHCHECK script send SIGTERM to PID 1 on failure so the container exits and `restart: unless-stopped` kicks in.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker: specify the exact log string, the conditions under which the loop emits it (e.g., unhandled exception, Gerrit API failure, rebase failure), and the code site in `infra/autolander/loop.py` that writes it — co-locate this definition with the AC3 observability spec so the loop implementation and `observability.sh` grep pattern are coupled.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker referenced in AC3 is never defined anywhere in the plan or the codebase: the plan says `observability.sh` greps for this marker, but specifies no emitting conditions, no log string, and no code site that writes it — unlike `VOTER_ERROR`, `MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR`, and `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` which are defined in the services that emit them.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: '`loop.py` emits `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` to stderr on any unhandled exception or Gerrit API error (≥3 retries exhausted); the `observability.sh` grep pattern is `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`. The loop uses `print("AUTOLANDER_ERROR ...", file=sys.stderr)` or `logger.error("AUTOLANDER_ERROR ...")`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `paused` state decision rule in AC7: specify whether setting the emergency-stop (a) completes the current `wipChain` iteration and then halts, (b) immediately stops and parks the wipChain, or (c) only prevents NEW selection while allowing the current wipChain to finish — and add `paused` to the S2 wipChain phase enum or explicitly state it is an overlay state that does not replace the phase field.
findings.finding: AC7's emergency-stop `paused` state has no defined decision rule for its interaction with an in-flight `wipChain`: the plan says it 'STOPS consuming `Autosubmit`' but does not specify whether an already-selected `wipChain` in phase `rebasing` or `awaiting_verified` is aborted immediately, allowed to complete, or parked — leaving the `paused` state's processing protocol undefined relative to the S2 `wipChain` phase enum.
findings.location: AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain): 'transitions the bot to a `paused` state that STOPS consuming `Autosubmit`'

findings.finding: The 'unhealthy' container state feeder described in AC3 relies on grepping journald (`CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1`) for the `unhealthy` state, but Docker's health-check state is a daemon-side flag — it does NOT appear as a log line in journald unless the HEALTHCHECK CMD explicitly writes a structured marker to stderr on failure. Without that specification, the observability.sh journald-grep pattern (used for VOTER_ERROR, MERGE_CHANGE_ERROR, etc.) cannot detect the 'unhealthy' state, leaving the second feeder of the autolander_errors metric silent during container hangs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the HEALTHCHECK spec (AC4), add: 'The HEALTHCHECK CMD must emit a structured stderr line (e.g. `echo AUTOLANDER_UNHEALTHY >&2`) on failure so the journald-grep feeder in observability.sh can detect it; or change the unhealthy-state feeder in observability.sh to poll `docker inspect --format {{.State.Health.Status}} compose-autolander-1` and publish 1 when the result is "unhealthy".' Either approach closes the gap; the plan must pick one and spec it explicitly.

findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker is referenced in AC3 as one of two feeders to the `autolander_errors` CloudWatch metric, but its emission contract is never defined: no code location in loop.py/the service specifies when to write it, no grep pattern is given for observability.sh, and the marker does not exist anywhere in the current codebase or in the S2 dependency ticket (0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec). An implementer of this story cannot wire the observability feeder without inventing the marker definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The loop service writes `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` to stderr on [list conditions, e.g., unhandled exception, submit failure after N retries]; `observability.sh` greps `CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1` for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` using the same offset-delta pattern as section 4.'

findings.finding: AC4 mentions 'autoheal/restart:unless-stopped' as the mechanism to restart the container on unhealthy state, but 'autoheal' refers to a third-party container (willfarrell/autoheal) that has zero existing call-sites anywhere in this codebase or compose stack. Introducing it would add a new external dependency without established precedent; alternatively, if the intent is only restart:unless-stopped (which does NOT fire on unhealthy, only on process exit), the hang-detection HEALTHCHECK goal is unmet.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart): 'an autoheal/restart: unless-stopped recreate it on exit/unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous 'autoheal/restart:unless-stopped' phrasing in AC4 with one of: (a) 'add a willfarrell/autoheal sidecar service to docker-compose.yml that watches HEALTHCHECK state and docker-restarts unhealthy containers' (with explicit justification for the new dependency); or (b) 'the HEALTHCHECK command exits 1 on hang AND the entrypoint wraps the main process with a timeout that causes a process exit, so restart:unless-stopped alone handles both crash and hang recovery' (no new dependency).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either scope AC7 down to drain-on-shutdown only (a deploy-correctness concern) and move the emergency-stop sentinel to a follow-on story, OR add a concrete observable proof for emergency-stop (e.g., 'a sentinel file present → poll loop logs `AUTOLANDER_PAUSED` within one iteration, verified in integration test') and for drain (e.g., 'on SIGTERM with an active wipChain, container exits only after logging `wipChain_acked`').
findings.finding: AC7 introduces an emergency-stop control-plane feature that is outside the stated scope ('Deploy + observability') and provides no end-state-observable proof: there is no specified test that the sentinel file causes the bot to enter a `paused` state and stop consuming Autosubmit labels, nor how drain-on-shutdown is verified (that the current wipChain completes before the process exits).
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC7: keep 'drain-on-shutdown on SIGTERM' here (it blocks safe first deploy) and add a verifiable signal (e.g., log tag `AUTOLANDER_DRAIN_COMPLETE` before exit, checked by the compose healthcheck or a test). Defer the emergency-stop sentinel to a follow-on story scoped as 'operational runbook controls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the CloudWatch representation for the 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' in AC6: either a StatisticSet (min/max/sum/sampleCount per period) or CloudWatch's Values+Counts distribution, with the concrete metric name, unit, and whether an alarm is required or it is dashboard-only.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies a 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' without specifying how the histogram is represented in CloudWatch PutMetricData. CloudWatch does not natively support histograms; the closest approximation is a StatisticSet (min/max/sum/sampleCount) or multiple individual metric values. The plan's use of 'histogram' without a specified representation leaves an implementer to invent a schema, which may be incompatible with any downstream alarm or dashboard the plan implies ('to validate the success criterion').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped`' ambiguity in AC4: explicitly specify whether an autoheal sidecar container (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal`) is required in the compose file to trigger restart-on-unhealthy, or document an alternative mechanism (e.g. a SIGKILL via health-timeout + process-exit → restart) that actually fires on hangs with plain compose.
findings.finding: AC4's liveness/restart mechanism has a correctness gap: plain docker compose 'restart: unless-stopped' does NOT restart a container that transitions to 'unhealthy' — it only restarts on process exit. The plan acknowledges this in parentheses but then writes 'autoheal/restart: unless-stopped' with a slash, leaving it ambiguous whether a dedicated autoheal sidecar is a required component of this story or merely an alternative. Without an autoheal container (or equivalent), a hung autolander is correctly detected as 'unhealthy' by the HEALTHCHECK and correctly feeds the autolander_errors metric, but it is NEVER restarted — the liveness goal is unachieved.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'an autoheal/`restart: unless-stopped`' with 'an `autoheal` sidecar service in docker-compose.yml (image willfarrell/autoheal or equivalent, watching AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autolander) — this is the mechanism that converts `unhealthy` state into a container restart under plain docker compose; `restart: unless-stopped` alone only covers process-exit.' Add the autoheal service to the Dockerfile/compose AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as an explicit edit target: extend `BOT_PATHS` (or add an `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` trigger) to include `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` and `infra/autolander/`, and decide/document whether the autolander gets its own health-check + rollback in the deploy loop.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: its `BOT_PATHS` variable governs which file changes trigger the auto-redeploy rebuild loop. `Dockerfile.autolander` (a new file in `infra/compose/`) is not in `BOT_PATHS`, so changes to it will not trigger a rebuild of the autolander container. Additionally, the autodeploy health-check logic and rollback logic are wired only to the `review-bot` service and its `/health` endpoint; the autolander service needs its own redeploy/health path or an explicit statement that manual deploys are acceptable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Decide and state the single heartbeat mechanism (file path **or** metric, not both) so S4 has an unambiguous contract to code against.
findings.finding: AC5 presents two implementation options ('a timestamp file in the state volume (or a health metric)') without deciding which one to use, forcing the executor to make a design choice mid-implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'The bot writes a UTC ISO-8601 timestamp to `<state-volume>/heartbeat` on every poll iteration; S4 reads this file path.' Remove the 'or a health metric' option.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC6: Specify the concrete mechanism for the queue-status surface (e.g., a JSON file at `<state-volume>/queue_status.json`, or a CloudWatch metric with specific dimensions) so the executor has an unambiguous target and a proving command can be written.
findings.finding: AC6 says 'emit a small queue-status surface' but does not specify the surface mechanism (log line, structured file in the state volume, HTTP endpoint, CloudWatch metric dimension), making the criterion un-implementable without a design decision by the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Example: 'The bot writes `<state-volume>/queue_status.json` on each poll with keys `wip_chain`, `waiting_count`, `phases`; the histogram is published as a CloudWatch metric `rebar/host:queue_wait_seconds`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Specify the HEALTHCHECK command (e.g., `CMD test $(( $(date +%s) - $(cat /state/heartbeat_epoch) )) -lt 120`), its `--interval`, `--timeout`, and `--retries` so the hang threshold is deterministic and verifiable.
findings.finding: AC4 states the HEALTHCHECK 'marks the container unhealthy on a hang' but never specifies what command the HEALTHCHECK runs, what process/endpoint it probes, or what timeout/threshold constitutes a 'hang' — leaving the executor to invent the implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Example: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=5s --retries=2 CMD test $(( $(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y /state/heartbeat) )) -lt 120` — marks unhealthy if heartbeat file is >2 min stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the autoheal dependency in AC4: either name and pin the specific autoheal image (with a digest), justify adding it as a new stack dependency, OR replace the hang-restart requirement with a watchdog approach that uses only existing primitives (e.g. the HEALTHCHECK exits the container on hang via a self-kill, letting `restart: unless-stopped` handle it natively without a sidecar).
findings.finding: AC4 casually couples 'autoheal' — a third-party Docker sidecar container — as the restart-on-unhealthy mechanism for the autolander. There are zero existing uses of autoheal in the docker-compose stack (the current docker-compose.yml has only gerrit and review-bot, both using plain `restart: unless-stopped` with no autoheal). This introduces a new, unnamed third-party image dependency with 0 prior call-sites in the codebase, solely to handle the HEALTHCHECK unhealthy→restart path.
findings.location: AC4 — 'a compose service + an autoheal/restart: unless-stopped recreate it on exit/unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Have the HEALTHCHECK script send SIGTERM to PID 1 on hang detection (e.g. `kill -TERM 1`) rather than just marking unhealthy — this causes the container to exit, triggering the existing `restart: unless-stopped` policy with no autoheal sidecar needed, consistent with the current stack's zero-extra-sidecar posture.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC7 drain-on-shutdown with the Scope boundary: either (a) clarify that 'finish the current wipChain' means only 'let the current poll tick complete and do not start a new one' (a deploy-layer SIGTERM concern, not loop logic), or (b) move the drain specification to S2/S3 where the loop logic lives and limit AC7 to 'handle SIGTERM cleanly.'
findings.finding: AC7's drain-on-shutdown requires the service to 'finish the current `wipChain`' before exiting on SIGTERM, but the Scope section explicitly states 'Not the loop logic.' Completing a wipChain iteration IS loop logic; implementing the drain means this story must own or invoke loop-processing behavior, directly contradicting the stated scope boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC7 drain clause as: 'On SIGTERM, the container sets a shutdown flag; the poll loop (owned by S2) checks this flag and exits after the current iteration completes — this story only requires the flag mechanism and SIGTERM handler, not the loop logic itself.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the CloudWatch histogram capability assertion in AC6: specify the exact emission mechanism — either (a) CloudWatch EMF via stdout (structured JSON log lines the CloudWatch agent ingests as distribution metrics), (b) client-side StatisticValues aggregation per interval, or (c) downgrade 'histogram' to a scalar summary metric (e.g. p50/p99 as separate gauges) — and confirm it is compatible with the existing PutMetricData-only pattern in observability.sh.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies a 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' emitted via `PutMetricData`, but CloudWatch `PutMetricData` does not natively support arbitrary histograms. The API accepts either a single `Value` (scalar) or `StatisticValues` (pre-aggregated Sum/SampleCount/Min/Max). True histogram distributions (percentile buckets) require either CloudWatch Embedded Metric Format (EMF) over stdout or the use of `StatisticValues` with repeated high-resolution calls. The plan does not verify which mechanism is intended or whether the target CloudWatch metric type (e.g. a CloudWatch alarm based on p99) is achievable with `PutMetricData` alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add queue-status surface and emergency-stop to the 'What'/'Scope' sections so every AC maps to a described deliverable, or (b) move AC6 and the emergency-stop clause of AC7 to the appropriate loop-logic story (S2/S3) where the poll loop lives.
findings.finding: AC6 (queue-status surface: wipChain details, waiting-change count, histogram metric) and the emergency-stop sentinel in AC7 appear as full acceptance criteria with no corresponding description in the 'What' or 'Scope' sections, making them orphan criteria relative to the plan's stated deliverables.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status surface), AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain) vs. 'What' and 'Scope' sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit failure-path test criteria for AC7: (a) drain-on-SIGTERM with a simulated hung wipChain must time out and exit cleanly within a specified deadline (not hang forever); (b) emergency-stop sentinel must take effect within at most one poll cycle; (c) SIGTERM during a blocked Gerrit call must still reach the handler — specify how each is verified (integration test, container-level test, or named follow-on ticket).
findings.finding: AC7 introduces two new operator-facing flows — drain-on-SIGTERM (finish current wipChain, ack, then exit) and emergency-stop (sentinel-file pauses Autosubmit consumption) — with clear, consequential failure paths (drain exceeds timeout/hangs indefinitely, SIGTERM not trapped, sentinel file not polled before next iteration), yet no failure-path tests are described for either flow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' in AC6 with a plain per-land-event timing gauge (e.g. `autolander_land_seconds` Count/gauge via the established `put-metric-data --unit Seconds` pattern), consistent with all existing observability.sh metrics and proportionate to a single-wipChain sequential lander.
findings.finding: AC6's 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' introduces a CloudWatch StatisticValues histogram — a pattern with zero existing uses in the codebase — into a single-instance sequential lander that holds exactly one wipChain at a time (S2 spec: 'Holds a single in-flight wipChain'). A histogram of sample-size N=1 per land event is statistically meaningless and far more complex to emit than the simple Count pattern every other metric in observability.sh uses (5 existing call-sites, all plain PutMetricData Count).
findings.suggested_fix: Emit `autolander_land_seconds` as a plain PutMetricData value (seconds taken from Autosubmit vote timestamp to submit) using the same `aws cloudwatch put-metric-data --unit Seconds --value $elapsed` shape as the existing metric sections — no StatisticValues/histogram needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the emergency-stop sentinel into a separate child story (e.g., 'S5b: Operational controls — emergency-stop + graceful drain'), leaving S5 scoped to deploy, single-instance guard, observability, and drain-on-SIGTERM only.
findings.finding: AC7's emergency-stop sentinel is independently valuable, independently testable, and independently releasable (it serves a distinct ops-team persona responding to incidents, and is not needed for the container to deploy and run correctly), making it a second outcome bundled into this deploy story without justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/end-to-end acceptance criterion for the SSM credential fetch: e.g., a smoke-test step in CI or a post-deploy validation that calls `fetch-secrets.sh` against a real (or localstack) SSM endpoint at the exact path `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` and asserts a non-empty, correctly-shaped token is returned — covering the failure path where the path or IAM grant is wrong.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString credential fetch via `fetch-secrets.sh` at path `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` is introduced as THE sole credential mechanism (a live AWS external boundary), yet the plan specifies no live/end-to-end acceptance test criterion for it. Any CI test that uses a mock/offline SSM substitute or skips the fetch entirely will bypass the actual IAM policy grant and SSM path, leaving the deployed credential path untested and allowing the ticket to close green while the live path is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'A post-deploy smoke test (or CI step using localstack) runs `fetch-secrets.sh` and asserts: (a) exit 0, (b) non-empty token written to the expected location; a separate negative test uses a wrong path and asserts non-zero exit + clear error message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Scope the autolander-bot's submit ACL to `refs/heads/main` (not `refs/heads/*`) in `All-Projects` or the target project's `refs/meta/config`, matching the bot's actual operational scope.
findings.finding: AC2 grants the autolander-bot 'submit permission on refs/heads/*' — this authorises the bot to submit changes to ANY branch on the Gerrit instance, not just `refs/heads/main`. The autolander's operational scope is restricted to landing changes onto `main`; granting submit on all heads is broader than the minimum required and increases blast radius if the token is compromised.
findings.location: AC2 — Credentials mechanism: 'plus submit permission on refs/heads/*'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Gerrit `refs/meta/config` access section, change the submit grant from `refs/heads/*` to `refs/heads/main` for the `autolander-bot` service user. If multi-branch landing is genuinely needed in future, add specific branch patterns rather than the wildcard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] E6: Add at least one concrete proving command per AC — e.g., for AC2: `docker run ... & docker run ... ; echo $?` shows exit-1; for AC3: `aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --metric-name autolander_errors ...`; for AC7: `docker kill --signal SIGTERM <container>` while a wipChain is active and assert log shows completion before exit.
findings.finding: No AC in the plan includes a concrete proving command (docker, aws, flock, grep, jq, etc.) to verify completion; every criterion is stated as a desired state with no described verification step, making done-ness unverifiable as written.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC clause (or extend AC3) to explicitly specify the CloudWatch log routing mechanism: the compose service's `logging` driver (e.g., `awslogs` with a named log group such as `/rebar/autolander`), so the 'stdout → CloudWatch' deliverable has a verifiable criterion.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes 'route logs to container stdout → the existing CloudWatch monitoring' as a deliverable, but no AC explicitly covers the CloudWatch log routing mechanism (log driver configuration, log group/stream names). AC3 covers only stdout and a PutMetricData metric — the log-stream-to-CloudWatch wiring is an uncovered deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md section) capturing the flock-over-replicas decision, the two-feeder metric design, the drain-on-SIGTERM contract, and the heartbeat-as-liveness signal so future maintainers understand the rationale.
findings.finding: Novel architectural decisions introduced by this story — the explicit rejection of `deploy.replicas: 1` in favour of a POSIX flock, the two-feeder single-metric design, the drain-on-SIGTERM contract, and the heartbeat-as-liveness signal — are not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Future maintainers encountering these patterns (especially the flock rationale) will not know why `replicas: 1` was deliberately excluded.
findings.location: AC1 (flock rationale), AC4 (drain-on-SIGTERM), AC5 (heartbeat), AC3 (two-feeder metric)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-autolander-deploy.md` (or a `## Autolander deploy decisions` section in CLAUDE.md) documenting: (1) why `deploy.replicas: 1` is insufficient for plain `docker compose`; (2) the POSIX flock as the sole guard; (3) the two-feeder `autolander_errors` metric; (4) heartbeat file as the liveness signal read by the HEALTHCHECK.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback section (or runbook reference) specifying the exact steps — at minimum `docker compose down autolander` plus the Terraform targets to destroy — to revert the compose service, SSM param, and CloudWatch alarm independently.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no explicit rollback procedure for the compose + Terraform (SSM param, monitoring alarm) changes if the deployment fails or the service misbehaves in production. While removing a new compose service is conceptually trivial, the Terraform-managed SSM param and CloudWatch alarm have no documented teardown sequence, leaving operators without a concrete, tested undo path.
findings.location: AC (overall) — Credentials (AC2 mechanism), Observability (AC3), and the Dockerfile/compose AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Rollback' subsection: (1) `docker compose -f infra/compose/docker-compose.yml stop autolander && docker compose rm autolander`; (2) `terraform destroy -target=aws_ssm_parameter.autolander_gerrit_token -target=aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.autolander_errors`; (3) remove the `Dockerfile.autolander` compose stanza and re-apply compose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` must have `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` added to the `rebar_secret_params` list as new work in this story's scope.
findings.finding: `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` does NOT currently contain `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token`. The plan's AC2 section says the token is 'added in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`', but the live file's `rebar_secret_params` list (17 entries) has no such entry. The phrasing presents the SSM slot as part of the design decision rather than clearly flagging it as new work in ssm.tf — a reader executing the plan must know to add this entry to the list.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token",` to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, with a comment analogous to the `reviewbot-tickets-pat` entry explaining its purpose and consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `monitoring_*.tf` glob reference with an explicit new filename (e.g., `infra/terraform/monitoring_autolander.tf`) and confirm it is new work this story must create, covering the `rebar/host:autolander_errors` metric alarm.
findings.finding: No `monitoring_*.tf` file currently contains a `rebar/host:autolander_errors` CloudWatch alarm. The plan's AC3 says 'with an alarm in `infra/terraform/monitoring_*.tf`', but the wildcard glob obscures that this is entirely new Terraform infrastructure; a grep across all monitoring files finds zero autolander references.
findings.suggested_fix: Name the file `infra/terraform/monitoring_autolander.tf` explicitly in AC3 and describe it as a new file (following the monitoring_s4b.tf / monitoring_autodeploy.tf pattern) that creates `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.autolander_errors` watching the dimensionless `rebar/host:autolander_errors` metric.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC3 that the `autolander_errors` alarm must set `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` (and optionally `ok_actions`) consistent with all other alarms in the repo.
findings.finding: The autolander_errors CloudWatch alarm specified in AC3 is not described as wired to the existing SNS alerts topic (`aws_sns_topic.alerts`). Every other alarm in the codebase (monitoring.tf, monitoring_s5.tf, monitoring_88ab.tf, monitoring_autodeploy.tf, monitoring_1fa8.tf) sets `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]`. Without this, the alarm transitions state but never sends a notification — it is observable in the CloudWatch console but silent to operators.
findings.suggested_fix: In the monitoring_*.tf alarm block for autolander_errors, add: `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` and `ok_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]`.

findings.finding: The plan states the Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but the existing script hard-codes `SSM_PREFIX="/rebar/prod"` and fetches a fixed, named set of leaf parameters — there is no autolander entry and no mechanism to fetch a parameter outside `/rebar/prod/`. The plan does not specify that the script must be extended.

The plan states the Gerrit HTTP token is 'fetched by `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`', but `fetch-secrets.sh` hardcodes `SSM_PREFIX='/rebar/prod'` and only reads a fixed set of named leaves under that prefix. The path `/rebar/autolander/gerrit-http-token` will never be fetched by the current script — it is silently absent from the generated `.env`, so the container starts without the credential even if the IAM scope issue is separately resolved.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `autolander_gerrit_http_token="$(get_param autolander-gerrit-http-token)"` to the fetch block in `fetch-secrets.sh` and `echo "AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_HTTP_TOKEN=${autolander_gerrit_http_token}"` to the `.env` write block. Then in `docker-compose.yml`'s autolander service, add `env_file: - .env` (matching the review-bot pattern).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
